title: Rapid Sampling of Interstitial Fluid from Human Skin Using Microneedles
author: Elizabeth C. Wilkirson
license_note: PMC full-text XML — Bio-protocol — CC-BY confirmed in article XML

steps.title: Dichloro-p-cyclophane (Parylene C dimer) (Specialty Coating Systems, CAS: 28804-46-
steps.instruction: Dichloro-p-cyclophane (Parylene C dimer) (Specialty Coating Systems, CAS: 28804-46-

steps.title: Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) Reagent Final concentration Quantity...
steps.instruction: Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume 184 silicone elastomer base n/a 40 g 184 silicone elastomer curing agent n/a 4 g Total n/a 44 g

steps.title: 70% isopropyl alcohol (IPA) Reagent Final...
steps.instruction: 70% isopropyl alcohol (IPA) Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume 2-Propanol 100% 70 mL Deionized water n/a 30 mL Total n/a 100 mL Laboratory supplies

steps.title: 42 mm diameter vacuum cup and...
steps.instruction: 42 mm diameter vacuum cup and hand pump (Hansol Medical Equipment, model: 2018-01-26-

steps.title: Use NX Student Edition software to...
steps.instruction: Use NX Student Edition software to create the design for the MN array according to the specifications below.

steps.title: Design a MN with a conical...
steps.instruction: Design a MN with a conical shape having a base diameter of 200 μm and height of 450 μm (Figure S1A).

steps.title: Create a 20 × 20 array...
steps.instruction: Create a 20 × 20 array of MNs with a needle-to-needle spacing of 400 μm on a 10 × 10 mm square base (thickness = 1 mm) (Figure S1B). See Note 1 for alternative MN design considerations.

steps.title: Load the
steps.instruction: Load the .stl file into Nanoscribe DeScribe software and convert the file to a .gwl file.

steps.title: Load the files into a Photonic...
steps.instruction: Load the files into a Photonic Professional GT+ lithography system and print the master using IP-Q resin and a 10× lens, following the manufacturer’s recommended procedure ( Figure 1A ) (Notes 2–3).

steps.title: After printing, fill one glass beaker...
steps.instruction: After printing, fill one glass beaker with 50 mL of SU-8 developer and another glass beaker with 50 mL of 100% IPA. Use tweezers to gently submerge the master in SU-8 developer for approximately 2 min, then submerge the master in 100% IPA for approximately 2 min.

steps.title: Inspect the master using a microscope...
steps.instruction: Inspect the master using a microscope to ensure that it was printed correctly and does not have any defects or imperfections (e.g., bubbles, cracks, or broken tips) (Troubleshooting Note 1).

steps.title: Use AutoCAD software to create a...
steps.instruction: Use AutoCAD software to create a 10 × 10 mm square to be used as a substrate for the master.

steps.title: Export the drawing to a laser cutter as a
steps.instruction: Export the drawing to a laser cutter as a .dwg file.

steps.title: Use the laser cutter to cut...
steps.instruction: Use the laser cutter to cut the substrate from a 3 mm thick PMMA sheet with double-sided adhesive attached to one side (Note 4).

steps.title: Adhere the substrate to the backside...
steps.instruction: Adhere the substrate to the backside of the master ( Figure 1B ). B. Fabrication of the master mold

steps.title: Place the master in the middle...
steps.instruction: Place the master in the middle of a Petri dish with the MNs facing upward.

steps.title: Combine 40 g of Sylgard 184...
steps.instruction: Combine 40 g of Sylgard 184 elastomer base with 4 g of curing agent in a mixing cup and stir the mixture vigorously for 2 min using a disposable stir rod.

steps.title: Place the PDMS mixture into a...
steps.instruction: Place the PDMS mixture into a vacuum chamber and open the vacuum valve (vacuum gauge level should go past 15 inch/Hg). Degas for 30 min (most of the air bubbles should be removed from the mixture).

steps.title: Pour the degassed PDMS mixture onto...
steps.instruction: Pour the degassed PDMS mixture onto the master in the Petri dish. Ensure the master is completely covered ( Figure 1C ).

steps.title: Place the Petri dish into a...
steps.instruction: Place the Petri dish into a vacuum chamber and open the vacuum valve (vacuum gauge level should go past 15 inch/Hg). Degas for 30 min or until all the air bubbles are removed from the mixture.

steps.title: Place the Petri dish in a...
steps.instruction: Place the Petri dish in a convection oven preheated at 80 °C for 2 h to cure the PDMS mixture.

steps.title: Remove the Petri dish from the...
steps.instruction: Remove the Petri dish from the oven and allow the cured PDMS to cool to room temperature.

steps.title: Use a razor blade to carefully...
steps.instruction: Use a razor blade to carefully cut out the cured PDMS from the Petri dish.

steps.title: Remove the master from the cured...
steps.instruction: Remove the master from the cured PDMS by slowly peeling it out and cut off excess PDMS to create the master mold ( Figure 1D ).

steps.title: Inspect the mold using a microscope...
steps.instruction: Inspect the mold using a microscope to ensure that it does not contain any defects or imperfections (e.g., bubbles, voids, or uncured PDMS) (Troubleshooting Note 2). C. Fabrication of the MN array replica

steps.title: Use tweezers to submerge the mold...
steps.instruction: Use tweezers to submerge the mold in 70% IPA for 30 min.

steps.title: Remove the mold from the IPA...
steps.instruction: Remove the mold from the IPA solution and let it air-dry for at least 1 h at room temperature.

steps.title: Place the dried mold on a...
steps.instruction: Place the dried mold on a flat, stable surface and pour SU-8 2025 photoresist into the mold, ensuring the mold cavity is completely filled ( Figure 1E ).

steps.title: Use tweezers to place the mold...
steps.instruction: Use tweezers to place the mold into a 50 mL centrifuge tube (Note 5). Ensure the MN tips are oriented toward the bottom of the centrifuge.

steps.title: Centrifuge the tube at 2,500× g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the tube at 2,500× g for 15 min ( Figure 1F ).

steps.title: Remove the mold from the tube...
steps.instruction: Remove the mold from the tube and place it under a UV lamp for 3 min to cure the SU-8 photoresist ( Figure 1G ).

steps.title: Remove the cured MN array replica...
steps.instruction: Remove the cured MN array replica from the mold using gloved hands ( Figure 1H ).

steps.title: Inspect the replica using a microscope...
steps.instruction: Inspect the replica using a microscope to ensure that it does not contain any defects or imperfections (e.g., missing or cracked tips) (Troubleshooting Note 3).

steps.title: Place the MN array replica into a parylene deposition system
steps.instruction: Place the MN array replica into a parylene deposition system. Load the system with 1 g of parylene C dimer and run the coating process following the manufacturer’s recommended procedure (Notes 6–7).

steps.title: Remove the parylene-coated MN array from...
steps.instruction: Remove the parylene-coated MN array from the deposition system and inspect it using a microscope to ensure that the array is completely coated and the coating is uniform ( Figure 1I ) (Troubleshooting Note 4). See Note 8 for guidance on batch fabricating MN array replicas.

steps.title: The MN array replica(s) can be...
steps.instruction: The MN array replica(s) can be stored in a Petri dish (MN tips facing upward) at ambient conditions. Figure

steps.title: Use AutoCAD software to create the...
steps.instruction: Use AutoCAD software to create the design for the skin patch according to the specifications below.

steps.title: Design the skin patch as a...
steps.instruction: Design the skin patch as a 42 mm diameter circle with a 2 × 2 array of 11 × 11 mm square cutouts (MN insertion sites) with 2 mm spacing (Figure S2). See Note 1 for alternative skin patch design considerations.

steps.title: Export the drawing to a laser cutter as a
steps.instruction: Export the drawing to a laser cutter as a .dwg file.

steps.title: Use the laser cutter to cut...
steps.instruction: Use the laser cutter to cut the patch from a 1.5 mm thick PMMA sheet with double-sided microfluidic tape attached to one side.

steps.title: Use a Dremel rotary tool to...
steps.instruction: Use a Dremel rotary tool to smooth the interior edges of the cutouts.

steps.title: Spray the patch with 70% IPA...
steps.instruction: Spray the patch with 70% IPA and wipe it clean using a Kimwipe to remove any debris.

steps.title: Use a laser cutter to cut...
steps.instruction: Use a laser cutter to cut medical-grade adhesive tape with the patch design to generate the skin patch sticker. E. ISF sample collection from human volunteers

steps.title: Remove the MN array from the...
steps.instruction: Remove the MN array from the Petri dish and rinse it with 70% IPA followed by air drying at room temperature.

steps.title: Wipe the anterior forearm of the...
steps.instruction: Wipe the anterior forearm of the volunteer using a fresh alcohol prep pad and allow it to dry (Note 9).

steps.title: Adhere the skin patch sticker to the cleaned forearm
steps.instruction: Adhere the skin patch sticker to the cleaned forearm.

steps.title: Load the MN array into an...
steps.instruction: Load the MN array into an MN applicator following the manufacturer’s instructions.

steps.title: Align the MN array over one...
steps.instruction: Align the MN array over one of the MN insertion sites and place the MN applicator on the sticker ( Figure 2A ). Press the applicator button to apply the MN array to the skin.

steps.title: Reload the MN applicator with the...
steps.instruction: Reload the MN applicator with the same MN array following the manufacturer’s instructions. Repeat step E5 two more times for a total of three MN applications per insertion site.

steps.title: Repeat steps E5–6 using the same...
steps.instruction: Repeat steps E5–6 using the same MN array at the remaining three insertion sites for a total of 12 MN applications (Note 10).

steps.title: Immediately following the completion of all...
steps.instruction: Immediately following the completion of all 12 MN applications, start a timer for 3 min (Note 11).

steps.title: While the timer is counting down,...
steps.instruction: While the timer is counting down, remove the backing from the sticker and adhere the skin patch to it, ensuring that the cutouts are aligned and that the patch is firmly attached to the sticker.

steps.title: While the timer is counting down,...
steps.instruction: While the timer is counting down, remove the backing from the skin patch and adhere the vacuum cup to it, ensuring that they are securely attached with no gaps between the cup and patch.

steps.title: After 3 min have passed, attach...
steps.instruction: After 3 min have passed, attach the hand pump to the vacuum cup and fully pull the handle back once to generate vacuum pressure inside the cup ( Figure 2B ) (Troubleshooting Note 5).

steps.title: Remove the hand pump from the...
steps.instruction: Remove the hand pump from the vacuum cup and maintain vacuum pressure for 20 min (Note 12) (Troubleshooting Note 5).

steps.title: Observe the cutouts of the skin...
steps.instruction: Observe the cutouts of the skin patch for the formation of droplets of ISF on the skin, which should appear ~5–10 min after vacuum application ( Figure 2C ) (Troubleshooting Notes 6–7).

steps.title: After 20 min have passed, release...
steps.instruction: After 20 min have passed, release the vacuum by pulling the pressure release valve on the top of the vacuum cup and detach the vacuum cup from the skin patch (Note 13).

steps.title: Use a capillary tube to collect...
steps.instruction: Use a capillary tube to collect the extracted ISF from the skin ( Figure 2D ).

steps.title: Remove the patch from the volunteer’s...
steps.instruction: Remove the patch from the volunteer’s forearm by slowly peeling one edge off the skin and using an adhesive remover pad to gradually peel away the rest of the patch.

steps.title: Wipe the sampling site using a fresh alcohol prep pad
steps.instruction: Wipe the sampling site using a fresh alcohol prep pad. Figure

steps.title: Transfer the collected ISF sample from...
steps.instruction: Transfer the collected ISF sample from the capillary tube into a low-bind microcentrifuge tube using a capillary plunger (Note 14).

steps.title: Incubate the sample for 1 h...
steps.instruction: Incubate the sample for 1 h at room temperature to allow any particulates to sediment.

steps.title: Centrifuge the ISF sample at 6,700×...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the ISF sample at 6,700× g for 10 min using a microcentrifuge.

steps.title: Use a micropipette to transfer the...
steps.instruction: Use a micropipette to transfer the supernatant to a new low-bind microcentrifuge tube.

steps.title: Snap-freeze the supernatant by immersing the...
steps.instruction: Snap-freeze the supernatant by immersing the microcentrifuge tube in liquid nitrogen for 5 min.

steps.title: Immediately store the snap-frozen sample at...
steps.instruction: Immediately store the snap-frozen sample at -80 °C until further analysis (Note 15).

description: Validation of protocol This protocol has been used and validated in the following research article: Jiang et al. [19]. Microneedle-based sampling of dermal interstitial fluid using a vacuum-assisted skin patch. Cell Reports Physical Science. The experimental procedure for fabricating the MN array via centrifugation-assisted replica molding is presented in Video S1, which results in the creation of mechanically robust and sharp MNs ( Figure 3A ). The strength of the MN array was validated through mechanical testing, as reported by Jiang et al. [19]. Force-displacement curves were generated for MN array replicas with different MN designs and array sizes, including the optimized MN design (base diameter = 200 μm, height = 450 μm) and array size (20 × 20) used in this protocol. This MN array can withstand up to 50 N of compression force, which is 1.5-fold larger than the force required to penetrate human skin, without exhibiting any signs of deformation ([19], Figure S1D). The penetration performance of the MNs was evaluated by inserting the MN array into porcine skin and performing histological analysis on skin sections, which revealed the formation of conical micropores in the epidermis ([19], Figure 2C ). Optical micrographs of the MN array after 12 skin insertions revealed that the MNs exhibited no discernible deformation or damage ([19], Figure S3D), validating the ability of the MN array to repeatedly penetrate human skin and generate micropores without fracturing or breaking. The ISF extraction efficiency was validated by sampling dermal ISF from 28 human volunteers, as reported by Jiang et al., [19]. ISF extracted using this technique was clear to light yellowish in color and generally more viscous than sweat ( Figure 3B -D). The average volume of ISF collected from all 28 volunteers was 20.8 ± 19.4 μL (mean ± SD) ( Figure 3E ). Studies evaluating the safety and tolerability of this technique revealed that it was well tolerated by all volunteers with only minor
